pattern: A valid cut only lands where a non-prime digit is immediately followed by a prime digit, so dynamic programming over those legal boundaries counts the partitions with a prefix-sum speedup.
hints: A piece is valid exactly when it begins on a prime digit and ends on a non-prime one, so think about which positions can ever start or end a piece.
A cut between index i-1 and i is only legal when i-1 is a non-prime digit and i is a prime digit; the whole string must also start prime and end non-prime.
Let dp[j][i] be the ways to split the first i characters into j valid pieces; sum dp[j-1][p] over legal start positions p at least minLength back, and accelerate that sum with a running prefix total.

edge_inputs.description: several valid partitions with min length two
tighter min length cuts options to one
min length one still constrained by digit types
string ends on a prime so no valid partition
single piece equals the whole valid string
k larger than possible pieces gives zero

stress.description: random short digit strings with small k and minLength
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 16)))
    s = ''.join(rng.choice('123456789') for _ in range(n))
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    minLength = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [s, k, minLength]
stress.note: kept tiny so the exponential oracle enumerating cut sets stays fast
